title: First to Third on a Single!

root.narration: Tied game, you're on first base with one out. The batter hits a clean single into right field! The ball gets past the infielders and is rolling toward the right fielder. Your third base coach is waving their arm in circles and yelling 'GO! GO! GO!' The right fielder is still running toward the ball. Do you stop at second or try for third?

choices.text: Go for third! My coach is waving me and the right fielder hasn't picked it up yet!
Play it safe and stop at second base.
I wasn't watching the coach. Just run and figure it out myself.

outcome-third-great.narration: You hit second base hard with your inside foot and keep running! The right fielder finally picks up the ball and throws, but you're already sliding into third! SAFE! You went first to third on a single! Now you're 90 feet from home with one out. A fly ball, a wild pitch, a ground ball -- so many ways to score! Your coach gives you a fist bump. That aggressive baserunning just changed the game!

outcome.headline: First to Third! Aggressive Running!
outcome.explanation: Going first to third on a single is one of the most impactful plays in baseball. It changes EVERYTHING. At second base, you need a hit to score. At third base, you can score on a fly ball, a wild pitch, a passed ball, a ground ball, a balk -- there are so many more ways to score! The keys to first-to-third are: (1) read the ball off the bat -- you knew it was a single to right field, (2) watch your third base coach -- they can see the outfielder and the throw, (3) hit the inside of second base with your foot so you make a tight turn. Trust your coach's waves!
outcome.whatToRemember: On singles to right field, always look to go first to third! Watch your third base coach and hit the inside of second base for a tight turn. Third base is SO much better than second!

keyTerms.definition: Going from first base all the way to third base on a single hit to the outfield. It's an aggressive play that puts you in scoring position with a short distance to home.
The coach standing near third base who tells you to stop, keep going, or slide. ALWAYS look at your coach when rounding second base!
When a runner is on second or third base. From scoring position, a single hit will usually score the runner. Third base is the BEST scoring position because you can score on so many plays.

outcome-second-okay.narration: You round second base and stop. You're safe at second, which is fine. But your third base coach was waving you around -- the right fielder bobbled the ball and a throw to third would have been late. You had a clear path to third but didn't take it. You're in scoring position, but at second base you need a clean hit to score. At third, you could have scored on a ground ball or a fly ball.

outcome.headline: Safe at Second, But Could've Had Third!
outcome.explanation: Stopping at second isn't wrong -- you're safe and in scoring position. But you left a base on the table. Your third base coach had the information you needed: the right fielder bobbled the ball, meaning the throw to third would have been late. That's exactly what the third base coach is for! They can see things you can't because you're running with your back to the outfield. When your coach waves you, TRUST THEM. The difference between second and third is huge -- at third you can score on so many more plays.
outcome.whatToRemember: Always look at your third base coach when rounding second! They can see the outfielder and they'll tell you to go or stop. Trust their judgment!

outcome-ignore-bad.narration: You're running hard but you never look at your third base coach. You round second base and glance out at the right fielder -- they already have the ball! You panic and try to go back to second, but you're past the bag. Now you're stuck between second and third! The right fielder throws to the shortstop and you're tagged out in a rundown. If you had looked at your coach, they would have told you to STOP at second. Instead, you got tagged out because you made the decision alone.

outcome.headline: Tagged Out! Didn't Watch the Coach!
outcome.explanation: Running without looking at your base coaches is one of the most common baserunning mistakes. When you're running from first to second, you should be looking at the ball. But when you ROUND second and are deciding whether to go to third, you MUST look at the third base coach. They're standing right there, watching the outfield, and they'll tell you exactly what to do: wave means go, hands up means stop. You tried to make the decision yourself, but you can't see the outfield while running toward third. That's literally why coaches stand there!
outcome.whatToRemember: When rounding second base, FIND YOUR COACH! The third base coach's job is to tell you whether to keep going or stop. Don't try to make that decision on your own.
